text: The surgical team uses a 10mm supraumbilical port for the camera, with two working ports placed in the left quadrant to avoid adhesions from a previous right subcostal incision.

The herniated transverse colon is visible within the Morgagni hernia. The hepatic flexure is adhered to the right upper quadrant abdominal wall. The surgeon reduces the hernia contents and divides the attachments of the hernia sac.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes as much of the falciform ligament as possible, preparing the area for the repair of the Morgagni hernia.
The surgeon observes the thin mesothelial lining within the hernia cavity, which is part of the hernia sac, but decides against excising this lining.

The surgeon begins closure of the Morgagni hernia by taking a bite at the mouth of the defect. A suture passer is introduced into the abdomen to grasp the other end of the suture, which is then pulled through the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon applies pledgets to the ender stitch and takes a second bite for the horizontal mattress suture. The needle end is cut, and the suture passer is used to pull the suture out onto the abdominal wall, engaging the pledget with the tissue.
The surgeon repeats the suturing maneuver until all sutures are applied, then ties down the transabdominal sutures to close the hernia defect.
The surgeon completes the takedown of the falciform ligament, reducing downward traction to achieve a tension-free primary repair of the hernia defect.
